4.1: ὁ κληρονόμος νήπιός ἐστιν (the heir is a minor/underage child, G3516) — nēpios denotes the child not yet of legal age, who possesses the inheritance legally but cannot access it independently. The analogy maps onto the OT period: Israel was heir to the promise but under guardianship, not yet in full possession.
4.2: ὑπὸ ἐπιτρόπους ἐστὶν καὶ οἰκονόμους (under guardians and trustees) — two classes of people managing the minor's affairs: epitropoi (guardians of the person) and oikonomoi (trustees of the estate). The law performed both functions: guarding conduct and managing the promise-inheritance before the fullness of time.
4.3: ὑπὸ τὰ στοιχεῖα τοῦ κόσμου (under the elemental spirits/principles of the world, G4747) — stoicheia is debated: elemental principles of learning (ABCs), elemental heavenly bodies, or elemental spiritual powers. The context (enslaved condition requiring divine redemption) suggests more than mere religious categories; the same term appears at v.9 for the powers the Galatians would return to.
4.4: ὅτε δὲ ἦλθεν τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ χρόνου (but when the fullness of time came, G4138) — plērōma tou chronou: not merely calendar timing but the completion/filling-up of the appointed period. History was moving toward this moment. ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ (God sent out his Son, G1821) — the compound exapostellō implies sending out from the divine sphere, presupposing the Son's pre-existence. γενόμενον ἐκ γυναικός, γενόμενον ὑπὸ νόμον (born of a woman, born under the law) — the two aorist participles mark the double contingency of the Incarnation: entering human nature and entering the specific covenant domain of the law.
4.5: ἵνα τοὺς ὑπὸ νόμον ἐξαγοράσῃ (to redeem those under the law, G1805) — the aorist subjunctive of purpose; exagorazō (to buy out of the marketplace) indicates payment and liberation from a condition of bondage. ἵνα τὴν υἱοθεσίαν ἀπολάβωμεν (that we might receive adoption to sonship, G5206) — apolabōmen (receive back/in full) suggests receiving what was always promised but not yet possessed. Huiothesia is the formal legal adoption-term, as in Rom 8:15,23.
4.6: ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (God sent out the Spirit of his Son) — the compound exapostellō is the same verb as v.4 for sending the Son; the Spirit's sending parallels the Son's sending. τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (of his Son) — the Spirit is identified as the Son's own Spirit, grounding the believer's filial cry in the Son's own relationship with the Father. κρᾶζον Ἀββά, ὁ πατήρ (crying Abba, Father, G2896) — the present participle describes ongoing crying; the Spirit's cry is continuous, not a one-time event.
4.7: ὥστε οὐκέτι εἶ δοῦλος ἀλλὰ υἱός (so that you are no longer a slave but a son) — the transition from slave-status to son-status is the direct consequence of the double sending (Son and Spirit) of vv.4-6. The address shifts to second singular: this is personal and specific, not merely corporate. κληρονόμος διὰ θεοῦ (heir through God) — the inheritance is now accessible through divine action rather than held in suspension under guardianship.
4.8: τοῖς φύσει μὴ οὖσιν θεοῖς ἐδουλεύσατε (you were slaves to those who by nature are not gods) — physis (G5449) marks the ontological claim: the pagan deities have no real divine nature. The enslavement was real but directed at non-entities; the more striking slavery would be returning to bondage of any kind after being freed (v.9).
4.9: νῦν δὲ γνόντες θεόν, μᾶλλον δὲ γνωσθέντες ὑπὸ θεοῦ (but now knowing God, or rather being known by God) — the correction (mallon de = but rather) is theologically significant: Paul prefers the passive construction to the active, since divine initiative precedes and grounds human knowing. The knowing is relational, not merely cognitive. πῶς ἐπιστρέφετε πάλιν (how are you turning back again?) — the present tense captures the in-process reversal; there is still time.
4.10: ἡμέρας παρατηρεῖσθε καὶ μῆνας καὶ καιροὺς καὶ ἐνιαυτούς (you observe days and months and seasons and years, G3906) — the calendar observances are symptoms rather than the disease; the disease is re-submission to law as a means of standing before God. The list moves from smallest to largest temporal unit, suggesting comprehensive calendar-compliance.
4.11: φοβοῦμαι ὑμᾶς (I fear for you) — the first person present expression of pastoral anxiety; Paul's fear is not about them as persons but about the possible futility of his previous labor among them. μή πως εἰκῇ κεκοπίακα εἰς ὑμᾶς (lest somehow I have labored in vain among you) — the perfect kekopika (I have labored) marks the sustained investment; the adverb eikē (in vain, without result) is the feared outcome.
4.12: γίνεσθε ὡς ἐγώ, ὅτι κἀγὼ ὡς ὑμεῖς (become like me, because I also became like you) — the reciprocal becoming: Paul became like Gentiles (freed from the law's jurisdiction) in his apostolic practice (cf. 1 Cor 9:21); now he calls them to become like him (standing in the freedom of Christ). The ethical argument is autobiographical before it is propositional.
4.13: δι᾽ ἀσθένειαν τῆς σαρκός (because of weakness of the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — here sarx is neutral: physical flesh, bodily weakness. The illness is not named but was Paul's occasion for first preaching in Galatia; the providential use of personal weakness is an implicit argument against flesh-reliance.
4.14: τὸν πειρασμὸν ὑμῶν ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου (your trial in my flesh) — Paul acknowledges that his bodily condition was itself a test for the Galatians: would they reject someone physically afflicted? ὡς ἄγγελον θεοῦ ἐδέξασθέ με, ὡς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (you received me as an angel of God, as Christ Jesus) — the reception was apostolic: through Paul's preaching they encountered Christ himself (cf. 4:19).
4.15: ὁ μακαρισμὸς ὑμῶν (your blessing/congratulation of me) — the term makarismos (G3108) refers to the previous beatitude/blessing the Galatians pronounced over Paul; the question "where is it now?" implies its disappearance. The hypothetical self-sacrifice ("you would have plucked out your eyes") suggests a particularly deep affection and commitment that has since cooled.
4.16: ἐχθρὸς ὑμῶν γέγονα ἀληθεύων ὑμῖν (have I become your enemy by speaking truth to you?) — the perfect gegona (I have become) asks whether a change has occurred in their perception of him; truth-speaking is the thing that produces enmity when the audience doesn't want to hear it.
4.17: ζηλοῦσιν ὑμᾶς οὐ καλῶς (they are zealous for you, but not in a good way) — the present tense of the agitators' ongoing activity; zēloō (G2206) is the verb for zealous pursuit; the "not well" qualifies the motive. ἐκκλεῖσαι ὑμᾶς θέλουσιν (they want to shut you out) — ekkleiō (G1576) is to shut out, exclude; the agitators want to cut the Galatians off from Paul's gospel-network so they become dependent on the agitators' alternative.
4.18: καλὸν δὲ ζηλοῦσθαι ἐν καλῷ πάντοτε (it is good to be zealously sought always in what is good) — Paul acknowledges that zeal-toward-someone is not inherently bad; the problem is the purpose and quality of the zeal. The "not only when I am present" (en tō pareinai me) refers to Paul's own presence: during his presence the Galatians' zeal was rightly directed.
4.19: τεκνία μου, οὓς πάλιν ὠδίνω (my little children, for whom I am again in labor pains, G5605) — the birth-pangs metaphor (ōdinō is the verb for labor-pains in childbirth) applied to apostolic travail; Paul is experiencing the same intensity as the initial evangelism. μέχρις οὗ μορφωθῇ Χριστὸς ἐν ὑμῖν (until Christ is formed in you, G3445) — the aorist passive subjunctive of morphoō: Christ taking shape/form within the community is the goal of the apostolic labor. The morphē language is the same root as Phil 2:6-7 (morphē theou/morphēn doulou).
4.20: ἠθελον δὲ παρεῖναι πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἄρτι (I wish I could be with you now) — the imperfect ēthelon indicates an unfulfilled desire in the present: he wants to be there but cannot. ἀπορούμαι ἐν ὑμῖν (I am at a loss about you, G639) — the present passive: Paul is in a state of perplexity about them, uncertain what to think. The honesty is pastoral rather than rhetorical.
4.21: λέγετέ μοι, οἱ ὑπὸ νόμον θέλοντες εἶναι (tell me, you who want to be under law) — the address is pointed: oî hypo nomon thelontes einai (those desiring to be under the law). The thelontes (wanting, present active participle) implies active desire, not passive entrapment. Paul then asks whether they know what the law says — a rhetorical challenge to attend to what the law itself teaches about being under it.
4.22: γέγραπται γὰρ ὅτι Ἀβραὰμ δύο υἱοὺς ἔσχεν (for it is written that Abraham had two sons) — the perfect gegraptai (it stands written) is the standard citation formula. The two sons structure the entire subsequent allegory: Ishmael from the slave woman (Hagar) by natural means, Isaac from the free woman (Sarah) by promise.
4.23: ὁ μὲν ἐκ τῆς παιδίσκης κατὰ σάρκα γεγέννηται (the one from the slave woman was born according to the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — kata sarka here means by natural biological means, without divine promise-involvement. ὁ δὲ ἐκ τῆς ἐλευθέρας δι᾽ ἐπαγγελίας (but the one from the free woman through promise) — the preposition dia specifies the mechanism: the promise was the means by which Isaac came into being.
4.24: ἅτινά ἐστιν ἀλληγορούμενα (which things are being allegorized/taken figuratively, G238) — the present passive participle: Paul is now reading the narrative figuratively, not claiming that the narrative is non-historical. αὗται γάρ εἰσιν δύο διαθῆκαι (for these women represent two covenants) — the two women are not merely private individuals but typological representatives of two covenant-administrations: Sinai (Hagar) and the promise (Sarah).
4.25: τὸ δὲ Ἅγαρ Σινᾶ ὄρος ἐστὶν ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ (now Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia) — the identification is geographical and typological: Hagar's territory is Arabia, where Sinai is located; the Sinai covenant belongs in the same symbolic geography as Hagar's bondage. συστοιχεῖ δὲ τῇ νῦν Ἱερουσαλήμ (she corresponds to the present Jerusalem, G4960) — the present Jerusalem (under Rome, under the law's continued claim) is in the Hagar-line, not the Sarah-line.
4.26: ἡ δὲ ἄνω Ἱερουσαλήμ (but the Jerusalem above) — the heavenly Jerusalem (cf. Heb 12:22; Rev 21:2) is in the Sarah-line: free. ἥτις ἐστὶν μήτηρ ἡμῶν (who is our mother) — the collective identification: believers are children of the free Jerusalem, not the enslaved one. The mother-city metaphor places their identity in the eschatological rather than the earthly Jerusalem.
4.27: The citation from Isa 54:1 (LXX) — εὐφράνθητι, στεῖρα ἡ οὐ τίκτουσα (rejoice, barren woman who does not bear) — describes the restoration of Zion after exile; Paul applies it to Sarah and to the Jerusalem-above: the woman who seemed barren (Sarah; the new covenant community) will produce more children than the apparently fruitful woman (Hagar; law-covenant Israel). The oti (because) introduces the reason: the barren woman will have more children.
4.28: ἡμεῖς δέ, ἀδελφοί, κατὰ Ἰσαὰκ ἐπαγγελίας τέκνα ἐσμέν (now we, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise) — the application to the present community: they are in the Isaac-line, children born of promise rather than of natural process. The identification with Isaac and the free woman is the ground of the freedom that follows in ch.5.
4.29: ὁ κατὰ σάρκα γεννηθεὶς ἐδίωκεν τὸν κατὰ πνεῦμα (the one born according to the flesh was persecuting the one according to the Spirit) — Paul identifies the Galatians' present situation with the Genesis pattern; the natural-birth line persecutes the promise-birth line. The hostility of circumcision-advocates toward Paul's uncircumcised-Gentile communities is this pattern repeated.
4.30: The Scripture citation (Gen 21:10) — ἔκβαλε τὴν παιδίσκην καὶ τὸν υἱὸν αὐτῆς (cast out the slave woman and her son) — is Sarah's command to Abraham, recorded in Genesis and treated as scripture's own word. οὐ γὰρ μὴ κληρονομήσῃ ὁ υἱὸς τῆς παιδίσκης μετὰ τοῦ υἱοῦ τῆς ἐλευθέρας (for the son of the slave woman will not inherit with the son of the free woman) — the double negative (ou... mē) is emphatic: absolute exclusion from shared inheritance.
4.31: διό, ἀδελφοί, οὐκ ἐσμὲν παιδίσκης τέκνα ἀλλὰ τῆς ἐλευθέρας (therefore, brothers, we are not children of the slave woman but of the free woman) — the conclusion drawn from the allegory: the community's identity is in the free woman's line. The absence of the article before eleutherας gives the characteristic a qualitative force: we are children of the one characterized by freedom, not bondage.

5.1: τῇ ἐλευθερίᾳ ἡμᾶς Χριστὸς ἠλευθέρωσεν (for freedom Christ freed us, G1659) — the dative tē eleutheria is probably a dative of purpose: Christ freed us for freedom. The cognate construction (eleutheria... ēleutherōsen) places freedom at both the beginning and the end: freedom is both the means and the goal. The aorist ēleutherōsen marks a definitive past act. στήκετε οὖν (stand firm therefore, G4739) — present imperative: continue standing. μὴ πάλιν ζυγῷ δουλείας ἐνέχεσθε (do not be entangled again in a yoke of slavery, G1758) — enechesthe (be entangled, be held in) is present passive imperative: stop being held in.
5.2: ἴδε ἐγὼ Παῦλος λέγω ὑμῖν (behold, I Paul tell you) — the emphatic personal assertion, using the same formula as the oath of 1:20; Paul stakes his personal authority on what follows. ὅτι ἐὰν περιτέμνησθε, Χριστὸς ὑμᾶς οὐδὲν ὠφελήσει (that if you are circumcised, Christ will benefit you nothing) — the future ōphelēsei (will benefit) is a statement of theological consequence, not a curse: choosing circumcision as the means of covenant standing removes one from the domain where Christ's benefit operates.
5.3: πάντι ἀνθρώπῳ περιτεμνομένῳ ὅτι ὀφειλέτης ἐστὶν ὅλον τὸν νόμον ποιῆσαι (to every person who lets himself be circumcised that he is obligated to do the whole law) — the present passive participle; accepting circumcision as a covenant obligation activates the entire legal system, not a single ritual. The law is not partial: one cannot selectively adopt its benefits without accepting its comprehensive demands.
5.4: κατηργήθητε ἀπὸ Χριστοῦ (you were severed/separated from Christ, G2673) — the aorist passive; the same katargeō verb Paul uses for the law being "nullified" is here used for severance from Christ. The irony: those seeking to add the law to their Christ-faith are severed from Christ. ἐκ χάριτος ἐξεπέσατε (you fell from grace, G1601) — the aorist of ekpiptō (to fall out of); grace is a domain and system from which one can exit by shifting to a law-basis.
5.5: ἡμεῖς γὰρ πνεύματι ἐκ πίστεως ἐλπίδα δικαιοσύνης ἀπεκδεχόμεθα (for we, by the Spirit, from/through faith await the hope of righteousness) — the two instruments (pneumati and ek pisteōs) specify how the awaiting happens: by the Spirit and through faith. Elpida dikaiosynēs (hope of righteousness) probably refers to the final eschatological verdict — the full realization of justification at the Last Day — rather than the present imputed status. The present tense apekdechometha (we keep on waiting) is the posture of those who live between the already and the not-yet.
5.6: ἐν γὰρ Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ οὔτε περιτομή τι ἰσχύει οὔτε ἀκροβυστία (in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any power, G2480) — the sphere en Christō Iēsou is the domain where the ethnic markers cease to be operative; within that domain, the only thing that matters is specified next. ἀλλὰ πίστις δι᾽ ἀγάπης ἐνεργουμένη (but faith working through love, G1754) — the present middle/passive participle; faith that is activated through love, or faith with love as the instrument of its activation. Love is not added to faith as a co-ground of salvation but describes the operative mode of genuine faith.
5.7: ἐτρέχετε καλῶς (you were running well, imperfect) — the progressive imperfect describes the previous sustained good running; the athletic metaphor indicates forward progress now interrupted. τίς ὑμᾶς ἐνέκοψεν (who cut in on you, G1465) — the aorist of enkoptō (to cut into, interrupt): someone has cut across their lane. The unnamed agitator is the one who interrupted the race.
5.8: ἡ πεισμονὴ οὐκ ἐκ τοῦ καλοῦντος ὑμᾶς (the persuasion is not from the one who calls you) — peismonē (G3988) is a hapax in the NT: persuasion, the quality of being persuaded. The persuasion the Galatians are following does not originate from the one who called them into Christ's grace (1:6).
5.9: μικρὰ ζύμη ὅλον τὸ φύραμα ζυμοῖ (a little yeast works through the whole batch of dough, G2219) — the proverbial statement (also 1 Cor 5:6) about the pervading power of small quantities; the principle applies to the law-adding agitation: a small doctrinal corruption spreads through the whole community.
5.10: ἐγὼ πέποιθα εἰς ὑμᾶς ἐν κυρίῳ (I am persuaded about you in the Lord) — the perfect pepoitha (I have been persuaded and remain persuaded): a standing confidence Paul maintains. ὁ δὲ ταράσσων ὑμᾶς βαστάσει τὸ κρίμα (but the one troubling you will bear the judgment, G941) — the singular ho tarassōn (the one troubling) individualizes the source of the trouble; bastasei to krima (will bear the judgment): the future tense points to eschatological accountability.
5.11: τὸ σκάνδαλον τοῦ σταυροῦ (the stumbling block of the cross, G4625/G4716) — if Paul were preaching circumcision, the offense of the cross would be eliminated; the cross as skandalon is precisely the stumbling block to those seeking a righteousness they can claim credit for. The cross eliminates all human achievement-grounds; that is its scandal.
5.12: ὄφελον καὶ ἀποκόψονται οἱ ἀναστατοῦντες ὑμᾶς (I wish those agitating you would go the whole way and castrate themselves) — the sarcastic irony: if circumcision is so important, why stop there? The rhetorical brutality serves the shock value of exposing the body-focused logic of the agitators by following it to its absurd conclusion. Apokopto (G609) means to cut off entirely.
5.13: ὑμεῖς γὰρ ἐπ᾽ ἐλευθερίᾳ ἐκλήθητε, ἀδελφοί (for you were called to freedom, brothers, G1657) — the aorist passive marks the definitive past calling; freedom is the domain into which the call brought them. μόνον μὴ τὴν ἐλευθερίαν εἰς ἀφορμὴν τῇ σαρκί (only do not use freedom as an opportunity for the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — aphormē (G874, base of operations, opportunity): freedom must not become a base from which the flesh launches its campaigns.
5.14: ὁ γὰρ πᾶς νόμος ἐν ἑνὶ λόγῳ πεπλήρωται (for the whole law is fulfilled in one word/commandment, G4137) — the perfect passive peplērōtai: a completed fulfillment that stands. The articular ho pas nomos (the whole law, with article) emphasizes comprehensiveness. ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν (you shall love your neighbor as yourself, Lev 19:18) — the neighbor-love command from Leviticus as the law's single comprehensive form.
5.15: εἰ δὲ ἀλλήλους δάκνετε καὶ κατεσθίετε (but if you bite and devour each other) — the animal-predation metaphor (daknō = bite; katesthiō = eat up/devour) describes the communal violence of competitive religious performance. βλέπετε μὴ ὑπ᾽ ἀλλήλων ἀναλωθῆτε (watch out lest you be consumed by each other) — the passive indicates mutual destruction through the very conflict that religious law-competition produces.
5.16: πνεύματι περιπατεῖτε (walk by the Spirit, G4151 disp=3) — the present imperative: an ongoing, habitual practice. The dative pneumati is instrumental: walking with the Spirit as the means and standard. ἐπιθυμίαν σαρκὸς οὐ μὴ τελέσητε (you will absolutely not fulfill the desire of the flesh) — the double negative (ou mē) plus aorist subjunctive is the strongest possible negation: you will under no circumstances fulfill the flesh-desire.
5.17: ἡ γὰρ σὰρξ ἐπιθυμεῖ κατὰ τοῦ πνεύματος, τὸ δὲ πνεῦμα κατὰ τῆς σαρκός (for the flesh desires against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh, G4561 disp=4/G4151 disp=3) — the mutual opposition is cosmic and structural; both sarx and pneuma use the same verb epithumeō (desire toward, desire against). ταῦτα γὰρ ἀλλήλοις ἀντίκειται (for these are opposed to one another, G480) — the present tense describes an ongoing structural opposition, not occasional conflict.
5.18: εἰ δὲ πνεύματι ἄγεσθε (but if you are led by the Spirit) — the present passive: being led is something happening to the person, not self-generated. οὐκ ἐστὲ ὑπὸ νόμον (you are not under the law) — the Spirit-led are not under the law because the Spirit is fulfilling the law's righteous requirement in them (cf. Rom 8:4); the law's guardianship function is superseded by the Spirit's direct leading.
5.19: φανερὰ δέ ἐστιν τὰ ἔργα τῆς σαρκός (the works of the flesh are obvious, G4561 disp=4) — phanera (manifest, evident): no special spiritual insight is needed to identify them. ἔργα (works) — the plural underlines the productivity of the flesh-principle; it generates a variety of destructive outputs.
5.20: εἰδωλολατρία, φαρμακεία (idolatry, sorcery/witchcraft) — pharamakeia (G5331) is the manipulation of spiritual powers through substances or rituals; both practices involve the replacement of God with alternative ultimate authorities. The social vices that follow (hatred, discord, jealousy, etc.) are equally "works of the flesh."
5.21: βασιλείαν θεοῦ οὐ κληρονομήσουσιν (will not inherit the kingdom of God) — the future with absolute negative: those who practice (prassontes, present participle of habit) these things are excluding themselves from the inheritance that Paul has been arguing is available through Christ and the Spirit. The warning is to those who continue in such practices, not to those who struggle against them.
5.22: ὁ δὲ καρπὸς τοῦ πνεύματος (but the fruit of the Spirit) — the singular karpos (fruit, G2590) contrasts with the plural erga (works) of v.19; the Spirit produces a unified, organic fruit rather than a catalog of separate achievements. The nine qualities listed are a single harvest, not a checklist. ἀγάπη χαρὰ εἰρήνη (love, joy, peace) — the first triad is relational; the second (patience, kindness, goodness) is dispositional; the third (faithfulness, gentleness, self-control) is volitional.
5.23: κατὰ τῶν τοιούτων οὐκ ἔστιν νόμος (against such things there is no law) — the dry understatement: no law prohibits the fruit of the Spirit. The law was given for restraining transgression; where the Spirit's fruit flourishes, the law's restraining function has no application.
5.24: οἱ δὲ τοῦ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ τὴν σάρκα ἐσταύρωσαν (those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh, G4717) — the possessive genitive tou Christou (belonging to Christ); the aorist estaurōsan marks a definitive past act, not an ongoing process: the crucifixion of the flesh is a completed event (cf. 2:20 "I have been crucified with Christ"). σὺν τοῖς παθήμασιν καὶ ταῖς ἐπιθυμίαις (with its passions and desires) — the flesh is crucified along with its characteristic outputs: the drives and desires that the vices of vv.19-21 express.
5.25: εἰ ζῶμεν πνεύματι, πνεύματι καὶ στοιχῶμεν (if we live by the Spirit, let us also walk in step with the Spirit, G4748) — stoicheō (G4748) means to march in a row, keep in step with a line; the metaphor is military/processional rather than the general "walk" (peripateō) of v.16. The subjunctive stoichōmen is a hortatory call to maintain alignment with the Spirit whose life one already has.
5.26: μὴ γινώμεθα κενόδοξοι (let us not become vainglorious, G2755) — kenodoxos = empty-glory-seeking; the present subjunctive ginōmetha is an ongoing prohibition: let us not become this. The three negative community-behaviors (empty boasting, provoking one another, envying) are the social pathologies that the flesh produces when the community forgets its shared Spirit-life.

6.1: ἐὰν καὶ προλημφθῇ ἄνθρωπός τινι παραπτώματι (if someone is caught/overtaken in some trespass, G4258) — prolambhanō may mean "caught in the act" or "overtaken, surprised." ὑμεῖς οἱ πνευματικοί (you the spiritual ones) — the adjective designates those who live by the Spirit (5:25); their responsibility for restoration is grounded in their Spirit-life. ἐν πνεύματι πρᾳΰτητος (in a spirit of gentleness) — the spirit/Spirit of gentleness: the same gentle quality named as a fruit of the Spirit (5:23).
6.2: Ἀλλήλων τὰ βάρη βαστάζετε (bear one another's burdens, G941) — the present imperative: an ongoing practice. καὶ οὕτως ἀναπληρώσετε τὸν νόμον τοῦ Χριστοῦ (and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ, G3551) — the future anaplērōsete (will fulfill, G378) has a different prefix from 5:14's peplērōtai; ana-plēroō means to fill up completely. The "law of Christ" (G3551 here in a positive sense) is not a new legal code but the governing principle of Christ's own self-giving (cf. 5:14: love your neighbor), described as "law" because it functions as the normative standard.
6.3: εἰ γὰρ δοκεῖ τις εἶναί τι μηδὲν ὤν (for if anyone thinks they are something when they are nothing) — the self-deception here is the inflation of self-estimation that enables judgment of others (v.1's "you who are spiritual") while exempting oneself. The present participle ōn (being nothing) is the corrective reality check.
6.4: τὸ δὲ ἑαυτοῦ ἔργον δοκιμαζέτω ἕκαστος (let each one examine/test their own work, G1381) — dokimazō is the test-and-approve verb; the examination is self-directed and specific to one's own ergon (work/action). εἰς ἑαυτὸν μόνον τὸ καύχημα ἕξει καὶ οὐκ εἰς τὸν ἕτερον (they will have ground for boasting in themselves alone and not in regard to another) — boasting grounded in comparative evaluation of others is eliminated; whatever ground for confidence exists is internal and self-referential.
6.5: ἕκαστος γὰρ τὸ ἴδιον φορτίον βαστάσει (for each one will carry their own load) — the different word from v.2's barē (heavy burdens): phortion (G5413) is the pack or standard load, not an exceptional weight. There is no contradiction with v.2; each person has their own appropriate individual responsibility before God, while the community bears each other's excessive burdens.
6.6: κοινωνείτω δὲ ὁ κατηχούμενος τὸν λόγον τῷ κατηχοῦντι ἐν πᾶσιν ἀγαθοῖς (let the one being instructed in the word share all good things with the one instructing, G2841) — the koinōneō verb indicates material sharing; the student-teacher relationship incurs a practical obligation of support. The logos (word) that is taught is the gospel.
6.7: μὴ πλανᾶσθε· θεὸς οὐ μυκτηρίζεται (do not be deceived: God is not mocked, G3456) — mykterizō (G3456) literally means to turn up one's nose at someone in contempt; God cannot be treated with contemptuous disregard. ὃ γὰρ ἐὰν σπείρῃ ἄνθρωπος, τοῦτο καὶ θερίσει (for whatever a person sows, that is also what they will reap) — the agricultural axiom of cosmic accountability: the harvest corresponds to the seed.
6.8: ὁ σπείρων εἰς τὴν σάρκα ἑαυτοῦ ἐκ τῆς σαρκὸς θερίσει φθοράν (the one sowing to their own flesh will reap corruption from the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — sarx here in its negative Pauline sense: the fallen human self-orientation. Sowing to the flesh is living to please the flesh-principle; the harvest is phthora (G5356, corruption/decay), the ultimate outcome of the Adamic existence. ὁ δὲ σπείρων εἰς τὸ πνεῦμα ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος θερίσει ζωὴν αἰώνιον (but the one sowing to the Spirit will reap eternal life from the Spirit) — the harvest of Spirit-oriented living is zōē aiōnios (life of the age to come): the eschatological life that the Spirit is the firstfruits of.
6.9: τὸ δὲ καλὸν ποιοῦντες μὴ ἐγκακῶμεν (in doing good let us not grow weary, G1573) — the present subjunctive hortatory: an ongoing encouragement to persevere. καιρῷ γὰρ ἰδίῳ θερίσομεν μὴ ἐκλυόμενοι (for in its own time we will reap if we do not give up) — kairo idio (in its own time): the harvest comes at the appointed time, not at the sower's preferred schedule; patience is essential to the agricultural metaphor.
6.10: ὡς καιρὸν ἔχομεν (as we have opportunity, G2540) — kairos is the appointed, opportune moment; the phrase acknowledges that opportunity is given, not perpetually available. τοὺς οἰκείους τῆς πίστεως (those of the household of faith) — the community of believers is named as the primary, though not exclusive, recipients of practical good.
6.11: ἴδετε πηλίκοις ὑμῖν γράμμασιν ἔγραψα τῇ ἐμῇ χειρί (see what large letters I write to you with my own hand) — Paul takes the pen from the scribe at this point (cf. 2 Thess 3:17; 1 Cor 16:21) to write a personal conclusion. The large letters may indicate poor eyesight (cf. 4:13-15), emphatic autograph, or both.
6.12: ὅσοι θέλουσιν εὐπροσωπῆσαι ἐν σαρκί (those who want to make a good showing in the flesh, G2146) — euprosōpeō (G2146, a hapax) means to appear well, put on a good face; the motivation is appearance management. μόνον ἵνα τῷ σταυρῷ τοῦ Χριστοῦ μὴ διώκωνται (only so that they might not be persecuted for the cross of Christ) — the ultimate motivation for the agitators is avoiding the social cost of the cross's scandal (5:11).
6.13: οὐδὲ γὰρ οἱ περιτεμνόμενοι αὐτοὶ νόμον φυλάσσουσιν (for not even those who are being circumcised keep the law themselves) — the present passive peritemnomenoi describes the ongoing circumcision-campaign; the exposure is ironic: the circumcision-advocates do not themselves keep the whole law they are requiring of others. ἀλλὰ θέλουσιν ὑμᾶς περιτέμνεσθαι, ἵνα ἐν τῇ ὑμετέρᾳ σαρκὶ καυχήσωνται (but they want you to be circumcised that they might boast in your flesh) — the flesh-boasting is the social credential of having brought Gentiles into circumcision.
6.14: ἐμοὶ δὲ μὴ γένοιτο καυχᾶσθαι εἰ μὴ ἐν τῷ σταυρῷ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (but far be it from me to boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, G4716) — the optative mē genoito is the strongest possible wish-rejection. The cross as the exclusive ground of boasting inverts the agitators' flesh-boasting. δι᾽ οὗ ἐμοὶ κόσμος ἐσταύρωται κἀγὼ κόσμῳ (through which the world has been crucified to me and I to the world) — both crucifixions are perfect passives: standing states; the world's claim on Paul and Paul's participation in the world's values have both been definitively ended by the cross.
6.15: οὔτε γὰρ περιτομή τί ἐστιν οὔτε ἀκροβυστία ἀλλὰ καινὴ κτίσις (for neither circumcision nor uncircumcision is anything but a new creation, G2537) — the two ethnic-religious markers are declared null in the sphere of the gospel (cf. 5:6; 3:28). Kainē ktisis (new creation) is the comprehensive eschatological category that replaces both: the new-creation work of Christ in the believer, not a minor religious modification.
6.16: τῷ κανόνι τούτῳ στοιχήσουσιν (as many as will march according to this rule, G2583) — kanōn (G2583) is the measuring standard or rule; the same stoicheō verb as 5:25 (walk in step with the Spirit). τὸν Ἰσραὴλ τοῦ θεοῦ (the Israel of God) — the phrase is debated: either all who follow the rule (Jew and Gentile = new covenant Israel) or specifically the Jewish believers who embrace the gospel. Given the letter's argument, it likely refers to all who are "in Christ" (3:29: "Abraham's seed"), which in Paul's usage constitutes the covenant community regardless of ethnicity.
6.17: τὰ στίγματα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ἐν τῷ σώματί μου βαστάζω (I bear on my body the marks of Jesus, G4742) — stigmata (G4742) are brand marks indicating ownership; slaves and devotees were branded. Paul's stigmata are the bodily scars from beatings and persecution suffered for the gospel of Jesus (2 Cor 11:24-25). The claim answers the agitators: he does not need the circumcision mark (flesh-based) because he already bears Jesus's marks (suffering-based).
6.18: ἡ χάρις τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ μετὰ τοῦ πνεύματος ὑμῶν, ἀδελφοί (the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers) — the closing benediction invokes grace from the specific person of Jesus Christ; the Spirit (pneuma) here is the human spirit of the Galatians, not the Holy Spirit. The blessing of grace upon the spirit rather than merely on the body or external circumstances matches the letter's entire argument: the new covenant is in the spirit and by grace, not in the flesh and by works.

1.1:
ἀπόστολος — "apostle" — taken in its strictest sense here; Paul is insisting on equality with the Twelve, not merely on a lesser missionary function. οὐκ ἀπ᾽ ἀνθρώπων οὐδὲ δι᾽ ἀνθρώπου (ouk ap' anthrōpōn oude di' anthrōpou) — the double negative covers both the origin (apo, "from" = source) and the channel (dia, "through" = intermediary): Paul's apostolate derives from no human community and was transmitted through no human intermediary. The opening sentence is the most polemically loaded salutation in the NT — the argument of the entire letter is compressed into the first clause.

ἀλλὰ διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ καὶ θεοῦ πατρός — "but through Jesus Christ and God the Father" — the coordinate pairing of Christ and the Father as the joint source of apostolic commission is an implicit Christological claim: Christ stands on the divine side of the origin of Paul's call.
1.2: ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις τῆς Γαλατίας (tais ekklēsiais tēs Galatias) — "to the churches of Galatia." Ekklēsia (assembly, church) is the Greek civic term for the assembly of citizens called together for public deliberation, applied to the gathered community of believers. The plural "churches" indicates multiple congregations across the Galatian region, all receiving the same letter — an unusual circular format suggesting the issue being addressed was not local but regional, a coordinated effort by the agitators across multiple communities.
1.3: χάρις ὑμῖν καὶ εἰρήνη — "grace to you and peace" — the standard Pauline greeting fuses the Greek epistolary opener (chairein = greetings, reshaped to charis = grace) with the Hebrew/Aramaic shalom (peace). The pairing is not merely formulaic: grace and peace name the content of the gospel — unmerited favor from God and its fruit in restored relationship. ἀπὸ θεοῦ πατρὸς ἡμῶν καὶ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — both God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ are presented as the joint origin of these gifts; the preposition apo (from) governs both, placing Christ coordinate with the Father as the source of saving grace.
1.4:
τοῦ δόντος ἑαυτὸν ὑπὲρ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν (tou dontos heauton hyper tōn hamartiōn hēmōn) — "who gave himself for our sins." The aorist participle dontos (having given) points to the single completed event of the cross; heauton (himself) emphasizes voluntariness — not something done to him but something he surrendered. The giving has a parallel in Titus 2:14 and the ransom-saying of Mark 10:45.

ὅπως ἐξέληται ἡμᾶς ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος τοῦ ἐνεστῶτος πονηροῦ — "to rescue us from the present evil age." Exaireō (to take out, rescue) has OT exodus resonance — God "taking out" Israel from Egypt. Aiōn ho enestōs ponēros (the present evil age) is the Jewish two-age schema: this age of sin and death is being displaced by the age to come, inaugurated in Christ's death and resurrection.
1.5: ᾧ ἡ δόξα εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — "to whom be the glory forever and ever." The doxology at the start of the letter rather than the end is unusual; Paul typically reserves doxologies for conclusions (cf. Rom 11:36; 16:27). Its placement here grounds the entire theological argument that follows in an act of worship: the rescue from the present evil age (v4) is itself the occasion for praise. The definite article hē doxa (the glory) — not merely glory in general but the pre-eminent divine glory proper to God alone.
1.6:
μετατίθεσθε (metatithesthe) — "you are deserting." The word is regularly used in Greek for a deserter in war, a political turncoat, or a religious apostate — the most charged available term for switching allegiance. The present tense is significant: the defection is in process, not complete. Paul writes to arrest a movement still underway. Thaumazō (I am astonished) expresses genuine shock, not merely rhetorical emphasis — the speed of the desertion is what stuns Paul: houtōs tacheōs (so quickly).

ἀπὸ τοῦ καλέσαντος ὑμᾶς ἐν χάριτι Χριστοῦ — "from the one who called you by the grace of Christ." The object of desertion is not a doctrine but a person — the God who called them. The Galatians' adoption of the other gospel is not merely theological error but a relational abandonment.
1.7:
ὃ οὐκ ἔστιν ἄλλο — "which is not another gospel" — more precisely, "which is not of a different kind." The Greek allo (another of the same kind) vs. heteron (another of a different kind) distinction is technically relevant: Paul has said heteron euangelion in v6 (another-different gospel); here he says it is not allo (not another-same gospel) — it is no gospel at all.

οἱ ταράσσοντες ὑμᾶς καὶ θέλοντες μεταστρέψαι τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τοῦ Χριστοῦ — tarassō (to stir up, agitate, disturb) — used for the agitation of water and civic disturbances. Metastrephō (to turn completely around, pervert) — stronger than "change": a radical inversion of the gospel's character.
1.8:
ἀνάθεμα ἔστω (anathema estō) — "let him be accursed." Anathema (Greek rendering of Hebrew cherem) is the most extreme form of religious exclusion: devoted to destruction, cut off from the covenant community and placed under divine curse. The twice-stated anathema — in vv8 and 9 — frames the warning with the legal weight of double witness (Deut 19:15). The hypothetical extreme — "even if we ourselves or an angel from heaven" — is not rhetorical flourish but the establishment of an absolute: no source of authority whatsoever can legitimate a different gospel.

The irony is sharp: the agent who preaches another gospel falls under the same curse-structure that the genuine gospel announces as Christ's fate on behalf of sinners (3:13: "cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree").
1.9: ὡς προειρήκαμεν (hōs proeirēkamen) — "as we said before." The "before" probably refers to Paul's previous visit — already at that stage the threat was visible and he had warned against it. The repetition now in writing doubles the witness: two formal statements, satisfying the Jewish-legal requirement of two witnesses to establish a matter (Deut 19:15). The anathema on the other-gospel-preacher is not personal anger but a legal verdict rendered twice for its binding force.
1.10: ἀνθρώπους πείθω ἢ τὸν θεόν (anthrōpous peithō ē ton theon) — "am I now seeking to please people, or God?" Peithō (to persuade, win approval, conciliate) targets the charge that Paul's law-free gospel was a people-pleasing accommodation to Gentile sensibilities — a softening of full Jewish covenant requirements to attract converts. The rhetorical question is anticipatory: the autobiography of vv11-24 will prove that his gospel came at enormous personal cost, not as a strategy for winning human favor. εἰ ἔτι ἀνθρώποις ἤρεσκον, Χριστοῦ δοῦλος οὐκ ἂν ἤμην — "if I were still trying to please people, I would not be a servant of Christ" — the present-unreal conditional establishes that the two postures are mutually exclusive.
1.11: τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τὸ εὐαγγελισθὲν ὑπ᾽ ἐμοῦ ὅτι οὐκ ἔστιν κατὰ ἄνθρωπον (kata anthrōpon) — "not of human origin" — literally "not according to the human standard or norm." Paul denies that his gospel fits the pattern of humanly-derived teaching, philosophical tradition, or institutional transmission. The passive euangelisthen (was proclaimed) hints at divine agency: the gospel was proclaimed through Paul but did not originate with him.
1.12: ἀλλὰ δι᾽ ἀποκαλύψεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (apokalypseōs Iēsou Christou) — "through a revelation of Jesus Christ." The genitive is likely both objective and subjective simultaneously: a revelation about Jesus Christ (its content) and a revelation from / given by Jesus Christ (its source). The Damascus encounter was simultaneously a disclosure of who Christ is and an act of divine self-disclosure by Christ himself. Apokalypsis (revelation, uncovering) contrasts with gradual human discovery: this was a sudden, unconditional divine disclosure that overturned Paul's entire prior framework.
1.13: τὴν ἐμὴν ἀναστροφήν ποτε ἐν τῷ Ἰουδαϊσμῷ (anastrophē) — "my former way of life in Judaism." Anastrophē (conduct, way of life, pattern of behavior) covers the entire complex of values, practices, and allegiances that constituted Paul's pre-conversion identity — not merely religious practice but who he was. Ioudaismos (Judaism) — a term Paul uses only here and v14 in the NT; it appears in 2 Maccabees for the distinctively Jewish way of life in opposition to Hellenistic assimilation. Paul does not disparage Judaism but describes his life as conducted within and for its advance.
1.14:
προέκοπτον ἐν τῷ Ἰουδαϊσμῷ ὑπὲρ πολλοὺς συνηλικιώτας (proekopton) — "I was advancing in Judaism beyond many of my own age." Prokoptō (to advance, cut a path forward, make progress) is an ambitious term implying swift movement toward a defined goal: Paul was not a passive participant but a high-achiever pressing past his contemporaries.

ζηλωτὴς ὑπάρχων τῶν πατρικῶν μου παραδόσεων — "extremely zealous for the traditions of my fathers." Zēlōtēs (zealot, one burning with zeal) connects Paul to the Phinehas-tradition (Num 25:11-13) of violent zeal for Torah-purity — the same zeal that drove his persecution of the church. The traditions of the fathers (patrikōn paradoseon) are the oral Torah, the interpretive framework built around the written law.
1.15: ὁ ἀφορίσας με ἐκ κοιλίας μητρός μου (ho aphorisas me ek koilias mētros mou) — "the one who set me apart from my mother's womb." Aphorizō (to set apart, separate, mark off) — the same root as Pharisaios (Pharisee = the separated one) and Paul's self-description in Rom 1:1 (aphorismenon eis euangelion = set apart for the gospel). The irony is profound: the Pharisee defined by separation from the impure discovers that God's prior act of separation was for the opposite purpose — to bring the gospel to the Gentiles, the very group from whom Pharisees separated. The prophetic echoes are deliberate: Jeremiah 1:5 ("before you were born I set you apart") and the Servant of Isa 49:1 ("before I was born the LORD called me") both shape Paul's self-understanding of a prenatal divine commission.
1.16:
ἀποκαλύψαι τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ ἐν ἐμοί (en emoi) — "to reveal his Son in me." The phrase is remarkable: not moi (to me, external vision) but en emoi (in me, interior transformation). The revelation was not merely visual data from outside but a life-reorienting disclosure within Paul's own being. This interior Christ is the theological ground of Paul's gospel: what he proclaims he received not as information passed on from others but as a living reality disclosed within him.

ἵνα εὐαγγελίζωμαι αὐτὸν ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν — the purpose clause connects the interior revelation to the exterior mission: the in-me disclosure of the Son was given for the sake of the out-to-the-nations proclamation. The two en phrases (in me / among the Gentiles) frame the missionary paradox.
1.17: οὐδὲ ἀνῆλθον εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα πρὸς τοὺς πρὸ ἐμοῦ ἀποστόλους — "I did not go up to Jerusalem to those who were apostles before me." The deliberate non-consultation is Paul's primary biographical evidence: he had the opportunity to receive the gospel from the Jerusalem apostles and declined it. Going "up to Jerusalem" (always the verb for travel to that elevated city) was the natural move for any new convert seeking authorization; Paul instead went to Arabia and Damascus. The independence is geographic as well as theological.
1.18: ἀνῆλθον εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα ἱστορῆσαι Κηφᾶν (historesai Kēphan) — "I went up to Jerusalem to visit Cephas." Historeō (to inquire of, visit for information, get acquainted with) — the term implies purposeful personal acquaintance rather than official consultation. Paul went to get to know Peter (Cephas), not to receive instruction from him or obtain apostolic authorization. The fifteen-day stay was social and informational; the brevity itself argues against an apprenticeship reading.
1.19: ἕτερον δὲ τῶν ἀποστόλων οὐκ εἶδον, εἰ μὴ Ἰάκωβον τὸν ἀδελφὸν τοῦ κυρίου — "I saw none of the other apostles — only James the Lord's brother." The identification ton adelphon tou kyriou (the Lord's brother) uses the standard Greek term adelphos (sibling), the same word used throughout the NT for Jesus's brothers (Mark 3:31; 6:3; John 7:3). Whether this means biological brother, half-brother, or cousin has been debated since the patristic period; the phrase leaves the Greek ambiguous but the most natural reading of adelphos without qualification is a full brother.
1.20: ἰδοὺ ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ ὅτι οὐ ψεύδομαι — "before God I assure you that what I am writing is no lie." The divine witness invocation — "before God" — is the functional equivalent of a formal oath. Its presence here signals the severity of the accusations being answered: Paul's opponents were claiming his gospel was dependent on Jerusalem and was therefore a deviant simplification when stripped of circumcision and food laws. The solemn attestation establishes his autobiographical account as legally binding testimony, not rhetorical self-defense.
1.21: εἰς τὰ κλίματα τῆς Συρίας καὶ τῆς Κιλικίας (klimata) — "the regions of Syria and Cilicia." Klimata (zones, sectors, geographical areas) suggests a broad regional itinerary rather than specific city visits. Cilicia includes Tarsus, Paul's hometown; Syria includes Antioch, which became his primary missionary base. The spatial independence reinforces the theological argument: Paul operated in regions geographically distant from Jerusalem's immediate sphere.
1.22: ἤμην δὲ ἀγνοούμενος τῷ προσώπῳ ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις τῆς Ἰουδαίας — "I was personally unknown to the churches of Judea." Tō prosōpō (by face, in person) specifies that the anonymity was physical: the Judean churches did not know his face. The imperfect ēmyn agnoumenos (I continued unknown) implies ongoing anonymity across the period. This seals the independence argument: not only did Paul not receive his gospel from the Jerusalem apostles, the very communities that would have transmitted Jerusalem tradition did not know him by sight.
1.23: ὁ διώκων ἡμᾶς ποτε νῦν εὐαγγελίζεται τὴν πίστιν ἥν ποτε ἐπόρθει — "the one who formerly persecuted us is now proclaiming the faith he once tried to destroy." The verbal chiasm — diōkōn (persecuting) and eporthei (destroying) reversed by euangelizetai (now proclaiming) — structures the conversion as a radical inversion. Portheō (to devastate, ravage, destroy) is Paul's own word for his pre-conversion activity (v13); the Judean churches now use it in their report about him. The reversal is so complete that it becomes itself the occasion for praise.
1.24: καὶ ἐδόξαζον ἐν ἐμοὶ τὸν θεόν (edoxazon en emoi ton theon) — "and they glorified God because of me." The imperfect edoxazon (they kept on glorifying) describes ongoing, continuous praise. En emoi (in me, because of me) — the same construction as 1:16 ("to reveal his Son in me"): the same interior-in-me that was the location of God's revealing act is now the occasion of God's glorification among the Judean believers. The transformed persecutor is not merely a biographical curiosity; his existence as a living gospel-proclaimer is an ongoing act of worship rising from the Jewish church.

2.1: διὰ δεκατεσσάρων ἐτῶν (dia dekatessarōn etōn) — "after fourteen years." Whether reckoned from Paul's conversion or from the previous Jerusalem visit (1:18) is one of the most discussed chronological puzzles in Pauline scholarship, with significant implications for the dating of Galatians and the relationship to Acts 15. κατὰ ἀποκάλυψιν (kata apokalypsin) — "according to revelation" — the divine-revelation language of 1:12 reappears: even this deliberate consultation was prompted by a direct divine revelation, not human pressure or hierarchy. The consultation is not a concession to Jerusalem authority but a revelation-guided coordination.
2.2:
ἀνεθέμην αὐτοῖς τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ὃ κηρύσσω ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν (anethemēn) — "I presented to them the gospel I proclaim among the Gentiles." Anatithēmi (to lay before, present for consideration) implies a collegial consultation, not a submission for approval: Paul laid out his gospel for mutual examination and understanding, not to receive authorization or endorsement of its content.

μή πως εἰς κενὸν τρέχω ἢ ἔδραμον — "lest somehow I was running or had run in vain." The athletic metaphor of running (trechō/trechein) in vain signals the practical concern: not that his gospel might be wrong, but that the two missions (circumcised and uncircumcised) might be working at cross-purposes in a way that would neutralize both.
2.3: οὐδὲ Τίτος ὁ σὺν ἐμοί, Ἕλλην ὤν, ἠναγκάσθη περιτμηθῆναι — "but even Titus, who was with me, was not compelled to be circumcised, even though he was a Greek." Titus is the test case: an uncircumcised Gentile believer physically present at the Jerusalem consultation, and not required to undergo circumcision. The aorist passive ēnankasthē (was not compelled) is the operative word — not that no one raised the issue, but that no compulsion was exerted. The pillar apostles' implicit approval of Titus's status is Paul's evidence that the Jerusalem leadership did not require circumcision as a condition of the Gentile mission.
2.4: διὰ δὲ τοὺς παρεισάκτους ψευδαδέλφους (pareisaktous pseudadelphous) — "because of the false brothers secretly brought in." Pareisagō (to bring in stealthily alongside) has the prefix para-eis- indicating infiltration: these individuals entered the gathering without proper standing. Pseudadelphos (false brother) is Paul's sharpest label — their claim of brotherhood is negated by their agenda. οἵτινες παρεισῆλθον κατασκοπῆσαι τὴν ἐλευθερίαν ἡμῶν — kataskopeo (to spy out, reconnoiter) is a military intelligence term; the intruders were scouts assessing the situation before a campaign to bring the Gentile mission under circumcision-requirement.
2.5: οἷς οὐδὲ πρὸς ὥραν εἴξαμεν τῇ ὑποταγῇ — "to whom we did not yield submission even for an hour." Eikaō (to yield, give way, capitulate) — the deliberate non-yielding: even temporary compliance would have been a concession in principle. "Not for an hour" is idiomatic emphasis: no partial accommodation, no provisional compliance pending clarification. The theological stake, stated in the purpose clause, is absolute: ἵνα ἡ ἀλήθεια τοῦ εὐαγγελίου διαμείνῃ πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "so that the truth of the gospel might remain with you" — the Galatians' own access to the gospel was at stake in Paul's resistance.
2.6: οἱ δοκοῦντες εἶναί τι (hoi dokountes einai ti) — "those who were considered to be something." The construction hoi dokountes (the reputed ones, those considered to be) is mildly ironic without being dismissive: Paul acknowledges the pillar apostles' reputation while refusing to let reputation function as theological authority. πρόσωπον θεὸς ἀνθρώπου οὐ λαμβάνει — "God shows no favoritism" — literally "God does not take the face of a person" (prosōpon... lambanō). This is the LXX idiom for partiality, translating Hebrew nasa panim (lift the face = show special favor). God's impartiality is the principle that subordinates reputation to truth.
2.7: πεπίστευμαι τὸ εὐαγγέλιον τῆς ἀκροβυστίας (pepisteumai) — "I have been entrusted with the gospel for the uncircumcised." The perfect passive pepisteumai (I have been entrusted and remain entrusted) marks the divine commissioning as completed and ongoing. The contrasting genitives — tēs akrobystias (of the uncircumcision) and tēs peritomēs (of the circumcision) — identify the target audiences, not two different gospels: the same gospel to two distinct mission fields.
2.8: ὁ γὰρ ἐνεργήσας Πέτρῳ εἰς ἀποστολὴν τῆς περιτομῆς ἐνήργησεν καὶ ἐμοί (energeō) — "the one who worked effectively in Peter for his apostleship to the circumcised worked effectively in me also." Energeō (to operate powerfully, work effectively) — the same verb in both clauses, emphasizing identical divine empowerment: the God who made Peter's apostolate effective made Paul's effective by the same means. The parallel structure places the two apostolicates in structural equivalence under the same divine agency — no hierarchy, no derivation of one from the other.
2.9: Ἰάκωβος καὶ Κηφᾶς καὶ Ἰωάννης, οἱ δοκοῦντες στῦλοι εἶναι (styloi) — "those reputed to be pillars." Styloi (columns, structural supports) is an architectural metaphor for those who bear the weight of the community: the pillars of the Jerusalem church. The order — James, Cephas, John — may reflect the authority structure of the early Jerusalem community, with James as its de facto president. δεξιὰς ἔδωκαν ἐμοὶ καὶ Βαρναβᾷ κοινωνίας — the right hand of fellowship (dexia koinōnias) was the formal gesture of recognized partnership and agreement in the Greco-Roman world: the pillar apostles acknowledged the Pauline mission as genuine and coordinate, not derivative.
2.10: μόνον τῶν πτωχῶν ἵνα μνημονεύωμεν — "only that we remember the poor." The single explicit condition of the Jerusalem agreement: material support for the poor of the Jerusalem community — probably the foundation of the collection that became a major Pauline project (1 Cor 16:1-4; 2 Cor 8-9; Rom 15:25-28). ὃ καὶ ἐσπούδασα αὐτὸ τοῦτο ποιῆσαι — "the very thing I was eager to do." Spoudazō (to be diligent, eager, make haste) — Paul embraced this obligation enthusiastically. The collection was not a burden imposed by Jerusalem but a chosen expression of the Gentile church's solidarity with the mother church.
2.11: κατὰ πρόσωπον αὐτῷ ἀντέστην (kata prosōpon autō antestēn) — "I opposed him to his face." Kata prosōpon (face to face, publicly) is the opposite of speaking behind someone's back; Paul confronted Peter directly and in front of the assembly. The incident probably belongs to the period described in Acts 15:35 — the interval after Paul and Barnabas's return to Antioch and before the second missionary journey. ὅτι κατεγνωσμένος ἦν — "because he stood condemned." Kataginōskō (to find fault, condemn, bring in a verdict against) in the perfect passive: the verdict against Peter's behavior was already rendered before Paul spoke — Paul's rebuke made public what was already true by the standard of the gospel.
2.12: πρὸ τοῦ γὰρ ἐλθεῖν τινας ἀπὸ Ἰακώβου μετὰ τῶν ἐθνῶν συνήσθιεν (synēsthien) — "before certain people came from James, he used to eat with the Gentiles." Synesthiō (to eat together with) — the table fellowship Peter maintained with Gentile believers was the practical expression of the equality-in-Christ that the gospel implied; sharing a meal was the strongest social bond available in the ancient world. ὅτε δὲ ἦλθον, ὑπέστελλεν καὶ ἀφώριζεν ἑαυτόν — hypostellō (to shrink back, withdraw) and aphorizō (to separate, set apart) — the Pharisaic separation vocabulary reenacted in the church; Peter re-enacted the wall the gospel had dismantled.
2.13: καὶ συνυπεκρίθησαν αὐτῷ καὶ οἱ λοιποὶ Ἰουδαῖοι (synypokrithēsan) — "and the rest of the Jewish believers joined him in his hypocrisy." Synypokrinomai (to act hypocritically alongside, play the same part together) — the theatrical image: actors performing a role that does not match their actual convictions. The compound verb (syn- = together) emphasizes the collective dimension: the hypocrisy was contagious and coordinated. ὥστε καὶ Βαρναβᾶς συναπήχθη αὐτῶν τῇ ὑποκρίσει — sunapagō (to lead away together with, be swept off course alongside) — even Paul's mission-partner was carried along by the social force of the crowd; the defection was not calculated but social-pressure-driven.
2.14:
οὐκ ὀρθοποδοῦσιν πρὸς τὴν ἀλήθειαν τοῦ εὐαγγελίου (orthopodousin) — "they were not walking in line with the truth of the gospel." Orthopodeō (to walk straight, walk with upright feet) — a hapax in Paul, probably coined from orthos (straight) + pous (foot): walking a straight line rather than a crooked one. The standard is not human propriety but "the truth of the gospel" — the gospel's own logic is the measure against which Peter's withdrawal was found deviant.

εἰ σύ, Ἰουδαῖος ὑπάρχων, ἐθνικῶς... ζῇς, πῶς τὰ ἔθνη ἀναγκάζεις ἰουδαΐζειν — the argument from Peter's own prior practice: if he himself had been living as a Gentile (without food-law separations), his withdrawal was not theological consistency but social fear. Ioudaizein (to Judaize, live in the Jewish manner) is the crux: Gentile believers were being effectively compelled to adopt Jewish practice as the hidden condition of full fellowship.
2.15: ἡμεῖς φύσει Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ οὐκ ἐξ ἐθνῶν ἁμαρτωλοί (physei Ioudaioi) — "we who are Jews by nature." Physei (by birth, by nature) marks the innate status as opposed to acquired status. "Gentile sinners" (ex ethnōn hamartoōloi) is the stock Jewish description of those outside the covenant — Gentiles were sinners by definition because they lived without Torah. Paul uses the phrase not to endorse its contempt for Gentiles but to work from a premise shared with his Jewish interlocutor: even those who began with Jewish birth-privilege have discovered that justification does not flow from that privilege.
2.16:
οὐ δικαιοῦται ἄνθρωπος ἐξ ἔργων νόμου ἐὰν μὴ διὰ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — "a person is not justified by the works of the law but through the faith/faithfulness of Jesus Christ." Pisteos Iēsou Christou — the genitive is debated: "faith in Jesus Christ" (objective genitive: human faith directed toward Christ — the traditional reading) vs. "the faithfulness of Jesus Christ" (subjective genitive: Christ's own faithful obedience as the ground of justification). Paul's subsequent argument in 3:10-14 — where Christ actively "becomes a curse" on behalf of others — tilts toward the subjective reading as at least a contributing sense; but both are theologically coherent, and neither is to be excluded.

Ergōn nomou (works of the law) — the specific practices that marked Jewish covenant membership and maintained its boundaries (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath, festival observance), not "good works" in the general moral sense. The triple repetition of the phrase in this verse (ex ergōn nomou... ex ergōn nomou... ex ergōn nomou) underscores how central the debate over these boundary practices was to the Galatian crisis.
2.17: εἰ δὲ ζητοῦντες δικαιωθῆναι ἐν Χριστῷ εὑρέθημεν καὶ αὐτοὶ ἁμαρτωλοί, ἆρα Χριστὸς ἁμαρτίας διάκονος; μὴ γένοιτο — "if, in seeking to be justified in Christ, we ourselves also are found to be sinners, is Christ then a servant of sin? By no means!" The argument: if Jewish believers who have abandoned law-keeping for faith in Christ are thereby "sinners" (because they have transgressed the law's framework by eating with Gentiles), does Christ — the one who led them to that transgression — become sin's servant? Mē genoito (the strongest possible negative: may it never be!) categorically rejects the conclusion.
2.18: εἰ γὰρ ἃ κατέλυσα ταῦτα πάλιν οἰκοδομῶ, παραβάτην ἐμαυτὸν συνιστάνω — "for if I rebuild what I demolished, I prove myself to be a lawbreaker." The logic is pointed: Peter tore down the wall of separation by eating with Gentiles; by withdrawing, he rebuilt it. The rebuilding retroactively declares the original tearing-down to have been a transgression. Peter's withdrawal does not protect him from the accusation of lawbreaking — it constitutes a self-condemnation of his previous inclusive practice. The one who goes back establishes the wrongness of going forward.
2.19: ἐγὼ γὰρ διὰ νόμου νόμῳ ἀπέθανον ἵνα θεῷ ζήσω — "for through the law I died to the law so that I might live to God." The prepositions are carefully distinguished: dying dia nomou (through, by means of the law) and dying nomō (to the law, in relation to the law). The law's own logic — Deut 27:26's comprehensive curse; Lev 18:5's unattainable demand — drives the honest observer to recognize that the law cannot produce the righteousness it demands. The law becomes the instrument of its own undoing for Paul: following it to its conclusion arrives at death, and through that death comes freedom from its claim. Χριστῷ συνεσταύρωμαι — "I have been crucified with Christ" — perfect passive: a completed co-crucifixion with permanent present effects. The same compound as Rom 6:6; the death with Christ is both past event and present reality of identity.
2.20:
ζῶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγώ, ζῇ δὲ ἐν ἐμοὶ Χριστός — "it is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me." The displacement of the egō by the indwelling Christ is the positive consequence of co-crucifixion: the old self is put to death, but the one living now is not a vacuum but the Christ-inhabited new self. The present tense zō... zē (I live... Christ lives) describes a permanent present state, not a momentary experience.

ἐν πίστει ζῶ τῇ τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ἀγαπήσαντός με καὶ παραδόντος ἑαυτὸν ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ — "I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me." The most intensely personal confession in Galatians: not "loved us" but "loved me" (agapēsantos me). The universal scope of the atonement is individualized in Paul's own experience; what Christ did for the world Paul claims as having been done for him specifically. This is not narcissism but the grammar of faith: the gospel must be received in the first person singular before it can be proclaimed in the first person plural.
2.21:
οὐκ ἀθετῶ τὴν χάριν τοῦ θεοῦ (atheteō) — "I do not nullify the grace of God." Atheteō (to set aside, declare void, render inoperative) is a legal term for nullifying a contract or reversing a verdict. To accept circumcision as a condition of justification would be to declare grace unnecessary — to nullify God's gift by claiming you do not need it.

εἰ γὰρ διὰ νόμου δικαιοσύνη, ἄρα Χριστὸς δωρεὰν ἀπέθανεν — "for if righteousness comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing." Dōrean (freely, without cause, to no purpose) — the same adverb as "hated me without cause" in Ps 69:4 / John 15:25. If law-keeping could produce righteousness, Christ's death was not merely unnecessary but gratuitous — an unjustified event in the cosmos. The logic cuts both ways: either Christ's death is necessary (because law-keeping cannot justify) or it was a meaningless waste.

3.1:
Ὦ ἀνόητοι Γαλάται (anoētoi) — "O foolish Galatians!" Anoētos (not using the mind, lacking understanding) — a censure of intellectual failure rather than moral failure; the Galatians are not wicked but foolish. This word differs from the term used for moral fools in Matthew 7:26 or 1 Cor 1:20; Paul charges not wickedness but failure to think through what they have experienced.

τίς ὑμᾶς ἐβάσκανεν — "who has bewitched you?" Baskaino (to bewitch, cast the evil eye, fascinate) — the only NT use of this term; it implies a malevolent external force has distorted their perception. οἷς κατ᾽ ὀφθαλμοὺς Ἰησοῦς Χριστὸς προεγράφη ἐσταυρωμένος — "before your very eyes Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified." Prographō (to write publicly, post, placard) — a public announcement posted for all to see; Paul's preaching of the crucified Christ was as vivid and public as a civic proclamation in the agora.
3.2: ἐξ ἀκοῆς πίστεως ἢ ἐξ ἔργων νόμου (akoēs pisteōs) — "from the hearing of faith or from the works of the law?" Akoē pisteōs can be read as (a) "the hearing that consists in faith" (faith-hearing, i.e., the act of receiving the message with trust) or (b) "the message that calls forth faith" (the faith-producing proclamation). Either reading makes the point: the Spirit arrived through the act of hearing and trusting the gospel, not through law-observance. Paul calls this a single argument sufficient to decide the case — the Galatians know how they received the Spirit, and law-keeping had nothing to do with it.
3.3: ἐναρξάμενοι πνεύματι νῦν σαρκὶ ἐπιτελεῖσθε — "having begun by the Spirit, are you now being completed by the flesh?" Enarchomai (to begin, make a start) and epiteleō (to complete, bring to full realization, perfect) frame the question as one of completion: the Spirit inaugurated the Galatians' Christian life; to seek law-keeping as its completion is to seek a fleshly fulfillment of what was spiritually begun. Sarki (by the flesh) here denotes human effort and achievement as the mode of completion, not moral evil — the problem is category, not character: seeking to perfect by human means what God began by grace.
3.4: τοσαῦτα ἐπάθετε εἰκῇ (epathete eikē) — "did you experience so many things in vain?" Paschō can mean either "to experience" (neutral) or "to suffer" (specifically negative). Both senses probably operate here: the Galatians' experiences include blessings (gifts of the Spirit, v5) and probable social suffering (persecution from their own Jewish communities for embracing Paul's law-free gospel). To adopt circumcision and law-keeping now would retroactively declare all of it meaningless — they suffered for nothing if law-keeping was the essential thing they were missing.
3.5: ὁ οὖν ἐπιχορηγῶν ὑμῖν τὸ πνεῦμα καὶ ἐνεργῶν δυνάμεις ἐν ὑμῖν (epichorēgōn) — "the one who supplies you the Spirit and works miracles among you." Epichorēgeō (to supply bountifully, provide at one's own expense) derives from the Athenian practice of the wealthy citizen (chorēgos) who funded the theatrical chorus; the image is of lavish patron-scale provision. God supplies the Spirit not grudgingly but with patron-like abundance. Energōn dynameis (working miracles, operating powerful acts) — the present participle describes ongoing miraculous activity in the Galatian communities at the time of writing.
3.6: Ἀβραὰμ ἐπίστευσεν τῷ θεῷ καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην — citing Gen 15:6 (the same text as Rom 4:3). The aorist episteusen (believed) points to the specific act of trusting the divine promise of Gen 15:5 (offspring as numerous as the stars). The aorist passive elogisthē (was credited, was reckoned) — God's forensic act in response: a reckoning of righteousness based on faith, not on circumcision (which came in Gen 17, after the faith-reckoning). Paul's argument in ch 3 deploys Gen 15:6 as the foundational proof that the Abrahamic principle was always faith, since the law came 430 years after Abraham (v17).
3.7: οἱ ἐκ πίστεως, οὗτοί εἰσιν υἱοὶ Ἀβραάμ — "those who are of faith are the sons of Abraham." Hoi ek pisteōs (the faith-people, those who derive their standing from faith) is Paul's compact counter-definition of Abraham's true heirs: not those from ethnic descent or circumcision-lineage but those from the same faith-principle by which Abraham himself was reckoned righteous. The hermeneutical move redefines the people of God without denying the Abrahamic covenant — it claims the covenant's deepest logic against those who read it as an ethnic entailment.
3.8: προϊδοῦσα δὲ ἡ γραφὴ... προευηγγελίσατο τῷ Ἀβραάμ (proeuangelizomai) — "Scripture, foreseeing... proclaimed the gospel in advance to Abraham." Proeuangelizomai (to pre-proclaim the gospel, announce the good news beforehand) — a verb Paul appears to have coined (or uses uniquely in this form); the gospel is not a NT invention but the fulfillment of a pre-proclamation already embedded in the Genesis text. The personification proiidousa hē graphē (Scripture foreseeing) presents the OT text as a divinely-sighted agent: Gen 12:3 ("all nations will be blessed through you") was foresight of the Gentile mission, not merely ethnic promise.
3.9: ὥστε οἱ ἐκ πίστεως εὐλογοῦνται σὺν τῷ πιστῷ Ἀβραάμ — "so then, those who rely on faith are blessed along with Abraham, the man of faith." Syn (together with, alongside) — the Gentile faith-people enter the Abrahamic blessing-stream not as separate recipients of a different promise but as sharers in the same blessing in which Abraham stands. Pistos Abraam (believing / faithful Abraham) — the epithet identifies Abraham by his defining characteristic: his trust. The Gentile believers share both the mechanism (faith) and the destination (blessing) with their Abrahamic prototype.
3.10: ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὃς οὐκ ἐμμένει πᾶσιν τοῖς γεγραμμένοις ἐν τῷ βιβλίῳ τοῦ νόμου — citing Deut 27:26. The curse requires doing everything (pasin) written in the law — partial compliance is still non-compliance. Emmenō (to remain in, continue in, abide by) targets the sustained, ongoing character of what the law demands: not a single act of obedience but uninterrupted, total compliance. The curse is therefore comprehensive: anyone who enters the law's framework takes on an obligation no human being has ever met, and falls under the curse the moment the perfect compliance breaks down.
3.11: ὅτι δὲ ἐν νόμῳ οὐδεὶς δικαιοῦται παρὰ τῷ θεῷ δῆλον — "that no one is justified before God by the law is clear." Dēlon (clear, evident, self-evident) — Paul treats this as derivable from Scripture itself, not merely from his argument. The proof is Hab 2:4: "the righteous will live by faith" — if faith is the principle of the righteous person's life, law-performance cannot be that principle. The two texts (Deut 27:26 and Hab 2:4) bracket the discussion: the law brings curse; the prophets establish faith as the source of life.
3.12: ὁ δὲ νόμος οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ πίστεως — "but the law is not from faith." The law and faith operate by different and logically exclusive principles: the law demands doing (poiēsas auta zēsetai = "the one who does them will live," citing Lev 18:5); faith receives as gift what it cannot produce. The two systems cannot simultaneously function as the mechanism of justification — one cannot both receive righteousness as a gift and earn it as a wage (cf. Rom 4:4-5). The law is not wrong but it is categorically different from faith.
3.13: Χριστὸς ἡμᾶς ἐξηγόρασεν ἐκ τῆς κατάρας τοῦ νόμου γενόμενος ὑπὲρ ἡμῶν κατάρα — "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us." Exagorazō (to buy out of, ransom from the slave market) — the redemption image: the law's curse had a price, and Christ paid it by bearing it himself. The opening of this verse has no connecting particle in Greek, giving it a staccato sharpness — a deliberate stylistic move that makes the statement land without diplomatic preparation. Genomenos hyper hēmōn katara (having become a curse on our behalf) — Paul says Christ actually became the curse itself, not merely that he suffered its effects: the one who bore it became what it was. The citation of Deut 21:23 (epikataratos pas ho kremamenos epi xylou = cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree) connects Roman crucifixion to the Deuteronomic curse-structure: the cross was not merely a method of execution but the form of the covenant curse.
3.14: ἵνα εἰς τὰ ἔθνη ἡ εὐλογία τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ γένηται ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ — "so that the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus." The double hina (purpose clauses) reveals that Christ's curse-bearing has two goals: (1) the Abrahamic blessing reaches the Gentiles, and (2) the promised Spirit is received through faith. The Spirit is identified as the content of the Abrahamic blessing in its new-covenant form: what the promise to Abraham ultimately delivers is not merely territory or national descendants but the Spirit who creates the new-covenant family across all nations.
3.15: κατὰ ἄνθρωπον λέγω — "I am speaking in human terms." The shift to an everyday legal analogy (kata anthrōpon = according to human practice) grounds the theological argument in accessible civil experience before the audience argues it has no relevance to divine dealings. ὅμως ἀνθρώπου κεκυρωμένην διαθήκην οὐδεὶς ἀθετεῖ ἢ ἐπιδιατάσσεται — "no one annuls or adds to a ratified human will/covenant." Kekyrōmenēn diathēkēn (ratified covenant, confirmed legal document) — the perfect participle indicates the permanent validity of the ratification; a sealed covenant cannot be unilaterally altered by one party under both Greek and Roman law. The a fortiori argument is unstated but obvious: if humans honor their ratified covenants, how much more must God's ratified promise stand inviolate.
3.16: τῷ δὲ Ἀβραὰμ ἐρρέθησαν αἱ ἐπαγγελίαι καὶ τῷ σπέρματι αὐτοῦ — "the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed." οὐ λέγει· καὶ τοῖς σπέρμασιν... ἀλλ᾽ ὡς ἐφ᾽ ἑνός· καὶ τῷ σπέρματί σου, ὅς ἐστιν Χριστός — "it does not say 'seeds,' referring to many, but 'your seed,' referring to one, who is Christ." Paul's exegesis of the singular sperma (seed) in Genesis deploys a rabbinic method: noticing the grammatical number of a word and drawing theological inference from it. This was a recognized interpretive technique in Second Temple Judaism. The argument identifies the ultimate referent of the Abrahamic promise not as a collective ethnic lineage but as the singular Seed-who-is-Christ — making the promise fundamentally messianic, not merely genealogical.
3.17: ὁ μετὰ τετρακόσια καὶ τριάκοντα ἔτη γεγονὼς νόμος οὐκ ἀκυροῖ — "the law, which came four hundred and thirty years later, does not annul the covenant." The 430 years from Exod 12:40 (the length of the Egyptian sojourn) establishes the chronological priority of the Abrahamic promise over the Sinaitic law: promise first, law second — and the later cannot retroactively invalidate the prior. Akuroō (to deprive of authority, invalidate, cancel) — the precise legal term for nullifying a prior legal instrument; the covenant-promise is not only prior but immune to retrospective annulment by any subsequent decree.
3.18: εἰ γὰρ ἐκ νόμου ἡ κληρονομία, οὐκέτι ἐξ ἐπαγγελίας — "if the inheritance is from the law, it is no longer from the promise." The two principles — law and promise — are mutually exclusive as the basis of inheritance; if one operates, the other cannot. τῷ δὲ Ἀβραὰμ δι᾽ ἐπαγγελίας κεκχάρισται ὁ θεός — "God graciously gave it to Abraham through a promise." Kecharistamai (perfect of charizomai) — the gift was given as an act of grace and the giving stands permanently: the perfect tense marks the irrevocability. Inheritance-by-law and inheritance-by-gift are not two versions of the same thing; they are structurally opposed mechanisms.
3.19: ὁ νόμος τῶν παραβάσεων χάριν προσετέθη — "the law was added because of transgressions." Prostithēmi (to add alongside, add in addition to something already existing) positions the law as supplementary, not foundational: it was added to the already-existing covenant-promise structure. Tōn parabaseōn charin (for the sake of transgressions) — the law's function in relation to sin is to define it as transgression, multiply it, and make it fully visible (cf. Rom 5:20; 7:7-8). ἄχρις οὗ ἔλθῃ τὸ σπέρμα — "until the Seed came" — the law had an explicitly temporary function, bounded by the arrival of Christ the Seed. διαταγεὶς δι᾽ ἀγγέλων ἐν χειρὶ μεσίτου — "ordained through angels by a mediator" — a Second Temple tradition (Deut 33:2 LXX; Acts 7:53; Heb 2:2) that the law was delivered at Sinai through the mediation of angels and Moses; the mediated, two-party character of the law contrasts with the directness of the divine promise given by God alone.
3.20: ὁ δὲ μεσίτης ἑνὸς οὐκ ἔστιν, ὁ δὲ θεὸς εἷς ἐστιν — "a mediator is not for one party alone, but God is one." The argument: mediation requires multiple parties (at minimum two) who must both perform their obligations. The promise, by contrast, was a unilateral divine declaration: God alone was the speaking party; no human response or compliance was required to make the promise valid. The law needed Moses as mediator and required Israel's obedience as the condition; the promise needed neither. Its validity rests entirely on God's own faithfulness, and God is one — undivided, unchanging, requiring no second party to ratify what he has declared.
3.21: ὁ οὖν νόμος κατὰ τῶν ἐπαγγελιῶν τοῦ θεοῦ; μὴ γένοιτο — "is the law then opposed to the promises of God? By no means!" The anticipated objection: if the law is temporary, supplementary, and unable to justify, does this make it God's enemy? Paul's answer is no — but the reason reveals the law's real scope: εἰ γὰρ ἐδόθη νόμος ὁ δυνάμενος ζωοποιῆσαι, ὄντως ἐκ νόμου ἂν ἦν ἡ δικαιοσύνη — "for if a law had been given that was capable of giving life, righteousness would certainly have come from the law." The contrary-to-fact conditional is decisive: no such life-giving law was given. Law and promise operate in different domains — the law defines sin and cannot produce life; the promise gives what the law requires and cannot supply.
3.22: ἀλλὰ συνέκλεισεν ἡ γραφὴ τὰ πάντα ὑπὸ ἁμαρτίαν (synekleis en) — "but Scripture locked up everything under sin." Synkleiō (to close in from all sides, hem in completely, shut up in an enclosure) — the image is of a shepherd closing his flock in a fold with no exit, or a net drawn tight around fish: the confinement is complete and there is no way out through one's own effort. Everything (ta panta) is enclosed — no exception for the especially devout. ἵνα ἡ ἐπαγγελία ἐκ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ δοθῇ τοῖς πιστεύουσιν — the purpose of the confinement is redemptive: the enclosure drives all toward the single available exit — the promise-gift given through faith in Christ.
3.23: πρὸ τοῦ δὲ ἐλθεῖν τὴν πίστιν ὑπὸ νόμον ἐφρουρούμεθα συγκεκλεισμένοι — "before this faith came, we were held in custody under the law, locked in." Phrouroumetha (imperfect passive: we were being kept under guard) — the military image of garrison troops keeping a population in custody; the law's role was that of a jailer maintaining a state of confinement. Sygkekleismenoi (locked in together, doubly enclosed) — the compound intensifies the image of total confinement. Crucially, the custody was not punitive for its own sake but prospective: eis tēn mellousan pistin apokalyphthēnai — "in anticipation of the faith that was about to be revealed." The lock-up was a holding pattern, not a permanent sentence.
3.24: ὥστε ὁ νόμος παιδαγωγὸς ἡμῶν γέγονεν εἰς Χριστόν — "so then the law was our guardian until Christ." Paidagōgos is not the teacher but the household slave who escorted a minor child to school, maintained discipline en route, and ensured the child arrived safely. The paidagōgos' function was temporary by definition — it ended when the child reached majority. Paul's metaphor is precise: the law's role was custodial and preparatory, not pedagogical in the permanent or instructive sense. Its task was to escort Israel to Christ, the destination; once arrived, the escort's function is complete. ἵνα ἐκ πίστεως δικαιωθῶμεν — "so that we might be justified by faith" — the purpose of the preparatory guardianship: leading to the justification-by-faith that the Christ-event makes possible and available.
3.25: ἐλθούσης δὲ τῆς πίστεως οὐκέτι ὑπὸ παιδαγωγόν ἐσμεν — "but now that this faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian." The aorist participle elthousēs (having come) marks the arrival of "faith" as a dateable historical event — the coming of Christ and the proclamation of his gospel. "No longer under a guardian" is the declaration of covenant maturity: the law's custodial role has ended for all who are in Christ. The Galatians who seek to take on the law's observances are going backward — seeking to return to minority status after the arrival of majority, to return to the escort-slave's hand after reaching the school.
3.26: πάντες γὰρ υἱοὶ θεοῦ ἐστε διὰ τῆς πίστεως ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ — "for you are all sons of God through faith in Christ Jesus." Pantes (all) — the universal scope is the point: all believers, without ethnic or social qualification, are sons/children of God. The status of covenant sonship that was previously the privilege of Israel-as-nation (Exod 4:22; Deut 14:1) is now available to all through union with the Son. Dia tēs pisteōs en Christō (through faith, in Christ) — the mechanism (faith) and the sphere (in Christ) are inseparable: sonship is not simply a new status assigned from outside but a relation entered by union with the one who is Son.
3.27: ὅσοι γὰρ εἰς Χριστὸν ἐβαπτίσθητε, Χριστὸν ἐνεδύσασθε — "for all of you who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ." Eis Christon ebaptisthēte (were baptized into Christ) — the preposition eis (into) marks entry into a sphere, a relation, a new domain; baptism is the rite of incorporation, not merely a rite of initiation. Enedysasthe (aorist middle: you clothed yourselves, you put on) — the baptismal clothing image: the physical act of removing garments before the water and being vested in white afterward enacted the theological reality of putting on Christ as a new identity-garment. The putting-on is not external costume but a change in whose life defines your life.
3.28:
οὐκ ἔνι Ἰουδαῖος οὐδὲ Ἕλλην, οὐκ ἔνι δοῦλος οὐδὲ ἐλεύθερος, οὐκ ἔνι ἄρσεν καὶ θῆλυ — "there is neither Jew nor Greek, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female." The three pairs cover the primary structural divisions of the ancient world: ethnic (Jew/Greek), socio-economic (slave/free), and biological (male/female). Note that the third pair shifts from oude... oude (neither... nor) to kai (and) — arsen kai thēly — possibly echoing the creation formula of Gen 1:27 ("male and female he created them"), suggesting that in Christ even the fundamental creaturely distinction is relativized in terms of standing before God.

Ouk eni (there is not, it does not exist) — an emphatic contracted form: these categories do not function as determinants of standing in Christ. They are not abolished as real distinctions; they do not determine one's access to God or one's spiritual status within the community.
3.29: εἰ δὲ ὑμεῖς Χριστοῦ, ἄρα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ σπέρμα ἐστέ, κατ᾽ ἐπαγγελίαν κληρονόμοι — "if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise." The syllogism completing the argument of ch 3: Christ is the Seed of Abraham (v16); those who belong to Christ share his identity; therefore they are Abraham's seed and heirs. The inheritance is kat' epangelian (according to promise) — not according to ethnic descent, not according to circumcision-status, not according to law-observance, but according to the original mechanism by which Abraham himself was reckoned righteous. The Gentile Galatians who are in Christ are Abraham's heirs in the fullest sense, because they stand on the same faith-ground on which Abraham stood before any of the national markers existed.
